agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services. The CEO describes it at a high level during an all-hands meeting — this is a vision statement, not a specification. The description is intentionally vague on thresholds, bounds, and implementation details, which means many critical business rules are implied but not explicitly defined.","bounded_context":"Pricing — specifically, the real-time calculation and adjustment of delivery prices based on demand signals. Intersects with Order Management (price presented at checkout), Demand Forecasting (input signal), and Financial Controls (guardrails/margins).","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand conditions."},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity. Undefined whether this is per-zone, per-time-window, or global."},{"term":"Delivery Price","definition":"The price charged to the customer for a delivery. The output of the pricing engine."},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or irrational pricing outcomes."},{"term":"Real Time","definition":"Pricing adjustments happen continuously or near-continuously in response to demand changes — not batch or scheduled."},{"term":"Fully Automated","definition":"No human approval is required for individual price adjustments within guardrail bounds."},{"term":"Competitiveness","definition":"Prices must remain attractive relative to market alternatives, implying a market-aware lower bound or benchmark."},{"term":"Profitability","definition":"Prices must cover costs and contribute to margin, implying a cost-aware lower bound — delivery must not be sold at a loss."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery price must increase when demand is high.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The direction of price movement relative to demand is a business policy. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during high demand for a promotion or strategic reason.","testable_assertion":"Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price must be greater than the base price."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery price must decrease when demand is low.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Low-demand discounting is a business policy choice, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand falls below the low-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price must be less than or equal to the base price."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time — prices reflect current demand conditions, not stale data.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The freshness requirement is a product policy. The business could decide to batch-update prices every 5 minutes and still have a valid system. However, stale pricing could lead to revenue leakage or customer distrust.","testable_assertion":"Given demand conditions change, when a delivery price is requested, then the price must reflect demand data no older than a defined staleness threshold."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Price adjustments must be fully automated — no human approval in the pricing loop.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is a product design decision. The business could introduce manual approval for certain thresholds or edge cases.","testable_assertion":"Given a demand change triggers a price adjustment within guardrail bounds, when the adjustment is applied, then no human intervention or approval step was required."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A price ceiling (upper guardrail) must exist to prevent prices from exceeding a maximum acceptable amount.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without an upper bound, an automated system could produce arbitrarily high prices, causing regulatory risk, reputational damage, and potential legal liability (price gouging laws). This is a system-integrity constraint — the system is corrupt if it can produce unbounded prices.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand level including extreme spikes, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price must not exceed the configured maximum price ceiling."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A price floor (lower guardrail) must exist to prevent prices from falling below the cost of fulfillment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Selling delivery below cost without explicit authorization corrupts financial integrity. An automated system that can price below cost indefinitely destroys the business. This is a data/state integrity constraint — the system must never produce a price that guarantees a loss.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand level including zero demand, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price must not fall below the configured minimum price floor."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The system must produce a valid, calculable price for every delivery request — it must never fail to return a price.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the pricing engine fails or returns no price, orders cannot be placed. This is an availability and state-integrity invariant — a pricing system that cannot price is broken.","testable_assertion":"Given any input conditions including missing or degraded demand data, when a delivery price is requested, then a valid numeric price is always returned."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Prices must balance competitiveness and profitability — neither goal fully overrides the other.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a strategic business policy that shifts with market conditions, growth phase, and executive priorities.","testable_assertion":"Given a calculated delivery price, then the price must be at or above the profitability floor AND at or below the competitiveness ceiling for the relevant market segment."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"Demand in a delivery zone exceeds the high-demand threshold","when":"A customer requests a delivery price","then":"The quoted price is higher than the base price for that zone, up to but not exceeding the price ceiling"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"Demand in a delivery zone falls below the low-demand threshold","when":"A customer requests a delivery price","then":"The quoted price is lower than or equal to the base price for that zone, but not below the price floor"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Demand spikes to an extreme level (e.g., 10x normal)","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price does not exceed the configured maximum ceiling (guardrail holds)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Demand drops to zero","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price does not fall below the configured minimum floor (guardrail holds)"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"The demand data feed is unavailable or stale beyond the freshness threshold","when":"A customer requests a delivery price","then":"The system returns a valid fallback price (e.g., base price) rather than failing or returning no price"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A price adjustment is triggered by demand change","when":"The adjustment is within guardrail bounds","then":"The price is applied automatically with no human approval step"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A price has been calculated","when":"The price is presented to the customer","then":"The price shown matches the price that will be charged (no bait-and-switch)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand data feed goes down entirely","expected_behavior":"System must fall back to a safe default price (base price or last known good price). Must never return zero, null, or an error to the customer."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly (flip-flopping between high and low)","expected_behavior":"System should have dampening or hysteresis to prevent price flickering that confuses customers and creates arbitrage opportunities."},{"scenario":"Price calculated at quote time differs from price at order confirmation time","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: honor quote-time price (price lock) or charge confirmation-time price. This is unspecified and critical."},{"scenario":"Simultaneous extreme demand across all zones","expected_behavior":"Price ceiling must hold globally. System must not allow cumulative surcharges to bypass the ceiling."},{"scenario":"Regulatory price-gouging event (e.g., natural disaster)","expected_behavior":"System needs an emergency override to cap or freeze prices. 'Fully automated' cannot mean 'no kill switch.'"},{"scenario":"Competitor drops prices dramatically","expected_behavior":"Competitiveness goal may conflict with profitability floor. Business must define which constraint wins."},{"scenario":"A single large customer (B2B) triggers demand spike","expected_behavior":"Contracted/negotiated pricing for B2B accounts may need to be exempt from dynamic adjustments. Not addressed in spec."},{"scenario":"Customer sees one price, clicks to order, price has changed","expected_behavior":"This is the classic race condition of dynamic pricing. A price-lock window or price guarantee period is essential for UX trust."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly impacts revenue and margin. Upside: capturing surplus during high demand and stimulating volume during low demand. Downside: misconfigured guardrails could cause revenue loss (floor too low) or demand destruction (ceiling too high). Unguarded automation is a financial risk vector.","operational":"Fully automated pricing removes human bottlenecks but introduces operational dependency on demand data feeds, pricing engine uptime, and guardrail configuration. Monitoring and alerting become critical. Configuration errors (e.g., wrong floor/ceiling) have immediate P&L impact with no human in the loop to catch them.","user":"Customers may perceive dynamic pricing as unfair ('surge pricing' backlash). Transparency in how prices are set, price-lock guarantees, and clear communication are essential for adoption. Without these, customer trust and retention suffer.","risk":"High. Automated pricing without well-defined guardrails carries regulatory risk (price gouging laws vary by jurisdiction), reputational risk (social media backlash on perceived unfairness), and financial risk (runaway pricing in either direction). The CEO's 'probably have some guardrails' is dangerously vague for a system with this risk profile.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected by price changes. May pay more during peak times. Need transparency and fairness guarantees to maintain trust."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"High demand periods with higher prices may increase earnings. Low demand periods may reduce available work. Compensation model interaction is unspecified."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Owns P&L. Needs visibility into pricing decisions, margin impact, and guardrail effectiveness. Needs audit trail."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Must monitor pricing engine health, demand feed quality, and guardrail configuration. New operational burden."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review guardrails against price-gouging regulations per jurisdiction. Emergency override capability is likely a legal requirement in some markets."},{"group":"Product/UX Team","impact":"Must design how dynamic prices are communicated to customers. Price transparency UX is critical for adoption."},{"group":"Sales/B2B Team","impact":"Contracted customers may expect fixed pricing. Dynamic pricing must not break existing contractual commitments."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting, not a product specification. Critical details are undefined: demand thresholds, price floor/ceiling values, staleness tolerances, price-lock behavior, jurisdictional rules, B2B exemptions, fallback strategies, and the exact meaning of 'competitive' and 'profitable.' The phrase 'probably have some guardrails' indicates guardrails are not yet decided, which is the single most important safety mechanism in the system. I can extract directional rules but cannot define precise, implementable acceptance criteria with confidence.","high_confidence_areas":["Price must go up when demand is high (explicitly stated)","Price must go down when demand is low (explicitly stated)","System must be automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though vaguely)"],"low_confidence_areas":["Guardrail values and whether they are hard invariants or soft preferences","Definition and measurement of 'demand' (per zone? per time window? global?)","Price-lock behavior (quote vs. charge time)","Fallback behavior when demand data is unavailable","Regulatory compliance requirements by jurisdiction","B2B/contracted customer exemptions","Courier compensation interaction with dynamic pricing","What 'competitive' means quantitatively"],"assumptions":["A 'base price' exists as the reference point for adjustments","Demand is measurable in real time via some existing or planned data feed","Guardrails include both a ceiling and a floor","The system operates per delivery zone, not as a single global price","Price gouging regulations may apply in some operating jurisdictions","Existing contractual pricing commitments must be honored"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Probably have some guardrails' — guardrails are the primary safety mechanism for an automated pricing system. Whether they exist, what form they take (hard caps, percentage limits, rate-of-change limits), and what values they have is completely undefined. This is the most critical design decision in the system.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Legal/Compliance","Customers","Operations"],"options":["A) Hard price ceiling and floor (absolute values)","B) Percentage-based caps relative to base price (e.g., max 2x, min 0.5x)","C) Rate-of-change limits (price cannot move more than X% per time window)","D) Combination of all three"],"recommendation":"D — use layered guardrails: absolute floor (cost of fulfillment), absolute ceiling (regulatory/reputational limit), and rate-of-change dampening. All three are needed for a safe system."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive but also profitable' — these goals can directly conflict. When competitors drop prices below our cost floor, which constraint wins? The spec provides no priority ordering.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Sales","Product"],"options":["A) Profitability always wins — never price below cost","B) Competitiveness can temporarily override profitability for strategic reasons","C) Escalate to human decision-maker when goals conflict"],"recommendation":"A as the default invariant, with C as an override mechanism for strategic exceptions requiring explicit human authorization."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"What constitutes 'demand'? Is it order volume, request volume, zone-specific capacity utilization, time-of-day patterns, or some composite signal? The pricing model's correctness depends entirely on this definition.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Data/Analytics","Operations"],"options":["A) Raw order count per zone per time window","B) Capacity utilization ratio (orders / available couriers)","C) Composite signal including forecasted demand","D) To be defined by data team"],"recommendation":"B — capacity utilization is the most meaningful signal for a delivery business because it captures both demand and supply. Raw volume alone ignores courier availability."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of price-lock behavior. In real-time pricing, the price at quote time may differ from the price at order confirmation. This is a critical UX and trust issue that must be defined before implementation.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Product/UX","Legal"],"options":["A) Price locked at quote time for N minutes","B) Price recalculated at confirmation time","C) Price locked but with a displayed expiry countdown"],"recommendation":"A — lock the price for a defined window (e.g., 5 minutes). Dynamic pricing that changes between quote and checkout destroys customer trust and may have legal implications."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of emergency override capability. Regulatory environments in many jurisdictions require the ability to freeze or cap prices during declared emergencies (natural disasters, public health emergencies). A 'fully automated' system without a kill switch may be legally non-compliant.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Operations","Customers"],"options":["A) Manual emergency override that freezes prices at base","B) Automatic detection of emergency conditions via external signals","C) Both"],"recommendation":"C — automatic detection with manual override as backup. This is likely a legal requirement, not optional."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
